headline: Microsoft Copilot Terms of Use Designate AI Output as 'For Entertainment Purposes Only'
deck: The legal disclaimer, last updated with a future date, raises questions about the reliability of artificial intelligence models amid a corporate adoption push.
body: Microsoft's artificial intelligence assistant, Copilot, operates under terms of use that explicitly state its output is "for entertainment purposes only." This particular designation has drawn criticism on social media, especially as Microsoft intensifies its efforts to secure corporate customers for the AI service.

The terms of use for Copilot, which appear to have been last updated on October 24, 2025, extend beyond a general disclaimer. They caution users that Copilot "can make mistakes, and it may not work as intended," further advising against reliance on the AI for critical guidance.
The full warning within the terms of use underscores the inherent limitations acknowledged by the developer:
“Copilot is for entertainment purposes only. It can make mistakes, and it may not work as intended. Don’t rely on Copilot for important advice. Use Copilot at your own risk.”

In response to the public discourse surrounding this language, a Microsoft spokesperson informed PCMag that the company intends to update these provisions. The current phrasing, described as "legacy language," no longer accurately reflects the contemporary applications of Copilot.
The spokesperson further elaborated on the company's position, indicating a planned revision:
“As the product has evolved, that language is no longer reflective of how Copilot is used today and will be altered with our next update,”

This practice of incorporating disclaimers regarding AI output reliability is not exclusive to Microsoft. Other prominent artificial intelligence developers, including OpenAI and xAI, also caution users against treating their generated content as definitive truth or a singular source of factual information. These broader industry warnings highlight a systemic acknowledgment among AI companies of the potential for inaccuracies within their models, prompting users to exercise caution and critical assessment of AI-generated content.

pipelineSteps.name: Source Discovery
pipelineSteps.detail: Aggregated technology news reports from TechCrunch, PCMag, and Tom’s Hardware detailing terms of service and company statements.

pipelineSteps.name: Fact Extraction
pipelineSteps.detail: Compiled specific clauses from Microsoft’s Copilot terms of use, official company statements, and comparative industry practices from OpenAI and xAI.

pipelineSteps.detail: Synthesized disparate media reports and corporate disclosures into a cohesive journalistic analysis of AI product disclaimers and their implications for user trust and corporate accountability.

pipelineSteps.detail: All specific factual claims, direct quotes, and numerical data, including the future date of the terms of use update and the corporate adoption push, were precisely verified against the provided fact sheet.
